title: Avgolemono Soup
subtitle: Greek chicken, rice, and egg-lemon soup
excerpt: Avgolemono is a Greek soup of chicken stock, rice, and a silky egg-and-lemon thickener that gives it a pale, creamy texture without any cream. It is mild, nourishing, and ready in under 30 minutes. The egg-lemon emulsion is what makes it distinctive.
sourceNotes: Avgolemono, meaning egg-lemon, is a technique used across Greek cooking both as a soup and as a sauce for vegetables and meats. The soup is one of the most widely made in Greece. A similar technique appears in Sephardic Jewish and North African cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The stock can be made ahead. Make the avgolemono finish just before serving; reheating after the eggs are added risks curdling.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Greek egg-and-lemon mixture used to thicken soups and sauces. Beaten eggs and lemon juice are tempered with hot broth, then stirred back into the pot to create a silky, creamy texture without any dairy.
To gradually raise the temperature of eggs or a sauce by slowly adding hot liquid to the cold mixture, preventing the eggs from scrambling on contact with the heat.

content.text: Avgolemono soup is a Greek chicken and rice soup thickened and enriched with a mixture of beaten eggs and lemon juice, the
emulsion. The result is a pale, silky soup with a clean lemon flavour, rich from the eggs but not heavy. It is ready in under 30 minutes and uses only a few ingredients.
The one thing to avoid is boiling the soup after the egg mixture goes in. High heat will scramble the eggs and curdle the soup. Keep the heat very low once the avgolemono is added.

items.prepNote: cooked and shredded, or leftover roast chicken

content.text: Bring {{chicken-stock}} of chicken stock to a simmer in a large saucepan. Add {{long-grain-rice}} of rice and cook for 15 to 18 minutes until the rice is tender. Add the shredded chicken and heat through for 2 minutes. Season with salt and pepper.
In a mixing bowl, whisk the eggs until pale and frothy, about 2 minutes. Whisk in the lemon juice.
Reduce the heat under the soup to very low. Ladle about 250 ml of hot broth slowly into the egg mixture, whisking constantly. This
the eggs so they don't scramble when added to the pot.

Pour the tempered egg mixture back into the pot in a thin stream, stirring as you go. Keep the heat very low and stir gently for 3 minutes. The soup will thicken slightly and become creamy. Do not let it boil. Serve immediately, scattered with parsley if you like.

items.symptom: The soup is grainy or the eggs have curdled
items.cause: Heat was too high when the eggs were added, or the egg mixture was not tempered first
items.fix: This can't be reversed once it happens. The tempering step is critical: always ladle hot broth slowly into the beaten eggs before adding to the pot, and always keep the heat very low.

items.symptom: The soup is not thick enough
items.cause: Eggs not whisked long enough, or too little rice
items.fix: Whisk the eggs until properly frothy and pale. The starch from the rice also helps thicken the soup; if it seems thin, let it sit off the heat for 5 minutes.

items.cause: Too much lemon juice for the stock used
items.fix: Add a little more stock to dilute. Start with the juice of 1.5 lemons and taste before adding the second.

content.text: Avgolemono soup is found on Greek family tables year-round and is a standard comfort food. The same technique appears in Greek cooking as a sauce for vegetables, stuffed vine leaves, and braised meats. It is also the classic way to finish a lemon lamb stew. The soup is best made and eaten immediately; it loses its silky quality if stored and reheated.
